one_line_takeaway: A cross-modal survey (not an original security study) that proposes a quality/trustworthiness metrics taxonomy for LLM-generated synthetic data and, via a curated representative-sample audit, finds that dimensions like faithfulness, safety, robustness, and benchmark contamination are far less consistently reported by existing generation-method papers than basic validity/utility metrics.
key_finding: For text data (Table 2, 8 audited works: FineWeb2, Dolma, RedPajama, Self-Instruct, RARR, PMI-DECODE, ORPO, Deng et al.), none explicitly report validity via dedicated metrics; faithfulness is explicit in only 2/8 (both evaluation-oriented works); safety is explicit in only 1/8 with 1 additional partial; benchmark contamination is explicit in only 1/8 with 1 additional partial. For symbolic/logical reasoning data (Table 3, 7 audited works: MetaMath, OpenMathInstruct-1, OpenCodeInstruct, UltraFeedback, ProofWriter, FaiRR, DyCodeEval), answer-level validity is explicit in 5/7, but faithfulness and benchmark contamination are each explicit in only 1/7 (partial in 1 additional each), and robustness is only partially covered in 2/7. The paper's overarching finding, replicated per-modality, is that intrinsic evaluation dimensions -- especially faithfulness, safety, robustness, and benchmark contamination -- are systematically under-reported relative to validity/utility in the generation-method literature the authors sampled, and that most existing surveys/evaluations rely on extrinsic (downstream task performance) rather than intrinsic data-quality signals.
core_contribution: This is a survey/taxonomy paper (published in TMLR, 06/2026; arXiv:2601.17717v3) titled 'A Survey on Evaluating Quality and Trustworthiness in LLM-Generated Data' (framed as the 'LLM Data Auditor'). The paper's own stated contribution is NOT a new method, dataset, or attack, but an organizing framework: (1) it characterizes how LLMs are used to generate data across six modalities (text; symbolic/logical reasoning; tabular; semi-structured graph/JSON/log; vision-language; agent data); (2) it proposes a metrics taxonomy of intrinsic evaluation dimensions split into two pillars -- Quality (validity, fidelity, diversity, utility) and Trustworthiness (faithfulness, safety, robustness, fairness, privacy, provenance, benchmark contamination); (3) using this taxonomy, the authors conduct a 'representative-work audit' -- annotating a curated (non-exhaustive) set of published generation methods per modality with a three-tier coverage scheme (checkmark = explicitly evaluated, triangle = partial/proxy coverage, x = absent) against the taxonomy's dimensions, to identify which evaluation dimensions are under-reported in practice; and (4) it discusses downstream usage patterns of synthetic data per modality and outlines future directions (dynamic feedback-loop evaluation, process verifiability, trust-utility trade-off analysis). The authors explicitly caveat that the audit is a 'comparative snapshot of a representative sample, rather than a field-wide prevalence estimate derived from an exhaustive meta-analysis.'
threat_model: None. This is a survey of evaluation methodology for LLM-generated synthetic data, not an attack/defense paper. It has no adversary model, no attack implementation, and no security experiments of its own. It does discuss (as cited background, not as its own contribution) security-adjacent topics that other papers have studied -- e.g., pretraining data safety filtering to reduce CBRN-uplift content (citing Anthropic, 2025), toxicity/jailbreak evaluation protocols (RealToxicityPrompts, ToxicChat), membership-inference and differential-privacy metrics for tabular/graph synthetic data (DCR, AUC-MIA, Adv-MIA, node-DP/edge-DP), benchmark contamination detection (TS-Guessing, retrieval-overlap audits), and watermarking/fingerprinting for provenance (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023 keyed statistical watermarks; RFEdit fingerprint injection). These are all Related Work citations organized into the survey's taxonomy, not findings the authors themselves generated.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: the audited paper set per modality is a curated, non-exhaustive 'representative sample' selected by the survey authors to cover method families rather than an exhaustive literature census, so the gap statistics (e.g., 'X of 8 works') are a snapshot of that sample, not a field-wide prevalence estimate. The coverage scoring (check/partial/absent) is a qualitative, author-applied annotation rather than an automated or independently verified measurement, introducing potential subjectivity/rater bias not discussed in the excerpted sections. As a survey, the paper synthesizes and re-frames existing metrics/methods but does not independently validate any of them empirically.

quantitative_results: Text data audit (Table 2, n=8 representative works): validity explicit in 0/8; faithfulness explicit in 2/8; safety explicit in 1/8 (partial in 1 more); benchmark contamination explicit in 1/8 (partial in 1 more)

Symbolic/logical reasoning data audit (Table 3, n=7 representative works): validity explicit in 5/7; faithfulness explicit in 1/7 (partial in 1 more); benchmark contamination explicit in 1/7 (partial in 1 more); robustness partial in 2/7, explicit in 0/7
